quiz.name: Session 07 Final Practice Quiz: Cyber Threat Hunting and Intelligence Gathering
quiz.description: Practice quiz with short-answer and essay questions. 5 short answer + 2 long answer questions. Use to consolidate learning before the final assessment.

name.text: Practice Q1: Hypothesis Formation for NTDS.dit Extraction

questiontext.text:
A threat hunter at a financial company reads a new report stating that a ransomware group is using scheduled tasks for persistence (ATT&CK T1053.005) and NTDS.dit extraction for credential theft (T1003.003).

Write ONE complete hunting hypothesis that targets the NTDS.dit extraction technique. Your hypothesis must include:

  • The threat scenario
  • The specific observable evidence you would look for
  • The data source where you would find this evidence
  • The time window for the hunt

Use the format: "If [actor/technique] is active in [environment/scope], I would expect to observe [specific evidence] in [data source] during [time window]."

graderinfo.text:
Marking Criteria (10 points):

  • 2 pts - Identifies specific technique (T1003.003 / NTDS.dit)
  • 2 pts - Identifies observable indicators (vssadmin shadow copies, ntds.dit file access, specific event IDs)
  • 2 pts - Names specific data sources (Event ID 4663, Sysmon EID 11, or similar)
  • 2 pts - Specifies target system type (domain controllers specifically)
  • 2 pts - Includes time window and overall hypothesis is testable

Model Answer: "If the ransomware group has established a foothold and is extracting credentials via NTDS.dit (T1003.003), I would expect to observe vssadmin.exe creating volume shadow copies followed by copy operations targeting the path %SYSTEMROOT%\NTDS\ntds.dit or the shadow copy path \?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy*\Windows\NTDS\ntds.dit, visible in Windows Security Event ID 4663 (file access auditing) and Sysmon Event ID 11 (file create) on all domain controllers over the past 30 days."

name.text: Practice Q2: Sigma Rule Selection vs Filter

questiontext.text:
Explain the difference between a Sigma rule's selection block and filter block. Give a specific example of when you would use a filter block in a rule designed to detect Mimikatz-style LSASS memory access.

Your answer should include a code example (YAML format) showing both a selection and filter block.

graderinfo.text:
Marking Criteria (10 points):

  • 3 pts - Correct explanation of selection (positive match criteria)
  • 3 pts - Correct explanation of filter (exclusion of known-legitimate)
  • 4 pts - Working YAML example with selection and filter for LSASS detection, with correct condition syntax (selection and not filter)

Key points: Selection = what to match (TargetImage ends with lsass.exe, suspicious GrantedAccess values like 0x1fffff). Filter = legitimate security products (MsMpEng.exe, SenseIR.exe, csrss.exe). Condition must be "selection and not filter".

name.text: Practice Q3: TLP Application to Incident Components

questiontext.text:
Your incident response team has just finished handling an intrusion. The IR report contains four types of information:

  1. IOCs of the attacker's infrastructure (IPs, domains, file hashes)
  2. The specific CVE that was exploited
  3. Your internal network architecture that the attacker mapped during discovery
  4. The name of the attacker group with medium-confidence attribution

For each of the four elements (a through d), specify which TLP level you would apply and briefly justify your choice (2-3 sentences each).

graderinfo.text:
Marking Criteria (10 points, 2.5 per element):

  • a) IOCs: TLP:AMBER (community sharing to enable blocking, but not fully public yet). Full marks for AMBER or GREEN with justification.
  • b) CVE: TLP:GREEN or TLP:CLEAR (CVEs are public; enabling community patching). Deduct 1pt for AMBER without strong justification (e.g., zero-day scenario).
  • c) Network architecture: TLP:RED (sensitive internal information; sharing helps attackers). Must be RED. Award 0 if any other TLP chosen.
  • d) Attribution (medium confidence): TLP:AMBER (trusted sharing with peers; not fully public due to attribution uncertainty and potential defamation risk).

Award partial credit for correct reasoning even if TLP level differs, as long as the logic is sound.

questiontext.text:
You are investigating a suspicious domain: support-auth-globalcorp[.]com.

Describe the step-by-step OSINT investigation process you would follow, naming at least 4 specific tools you would use and explaining what specific information you would seek from each tool.

Also note any operational security considerations you should take during this investigation.

graderinfo.text:
Marking Criteria (10 points):

  • 6 pts - 4+ tools named with specific queries/information sought (1.5 per tool): WHOIS (age, registrar), VirusTotal (detections, resolutions), crt.sh (subdomains, cert patterns), Shodan (ports, services), URLScan (page content, links), OTX (community reports)
  • 2 pts - Logical investigation sequence (start passive, escalate based on findings)
  • 2 pts - Operational security mentioned: don't visit URL directly, use isolated VM/network, be aware server may log your IP

Deduct 1pt if student mentions actively attacking or scanning the domain (illegal).

name.text: Practice Q5: Intelligence Types for CISO Questions

questiontext.text:
A CISO asks your threat intelligence team to answer three questions:

  1. "Are any of our employee email addresses currently in criminal marketplaces?"
  2. "What TTPs is the FIN7 ransomware group currently using in our sector?"
  3. "What is the overall ransomware threat trend for healthcare organizations over the next 18 months?"

For each question, identify whether it requires tactical, operational, or strategic intelligence, and describe what the intelligence product should look like (format, audience, frequency, content).

graderinfo.text:
Marking Criteria (10 points, ~3 per question + 1 for overall quality):

  • Q1 - Tactical: Specific compromised credentials require immediate action (Flash Alert, < 4 hour delivery, SOC + identity team audience, one-time or ongoing monitoring). Full credit: correctly identifies tactical + describes actionable flash format.
  • Q2 - Operational: Current actor campaign activity = Threat Actor Brief (2-4 pages, weekly, hunters + SOC lead audience, ATT&CK mapped TTPs + detection recommendations). Full credit: correctly identifies operational + describes threat actor brief format.
  • Q3 - Strategic: 18-month trend = Threat Landscape Assessment (quarterly report, 5-10 pages, CISO + board audience, sector trends, strategic recommendations). Full credit: correctly identifies strategic + describes landscape report format.

Note: Some students may argue Q2 is also strategic; accept if justification is sound.

name.text: Practice Q6: Data-Driven Hunt Scenario - MSIExec Spawning PowerShell

questiontext.text:
Your SOC analyst has flagged an unusual pattern during a routine review. A stack-counting query across 90 days of data includes this row:

ParentImage: C:\Windows\SysWOW64\msiexec.exe
Image:       C:\Windows\System32\powershell.exe
Count:       1

The analyst also found Sysmon EventID=3 (NetworkConnect) records showing this PowerShell instance connected to 45.89.125.203:443 exactly 30 seconds after launch.

Answer all four parts:

a) Is this suspicious? Explain your reasoning step by step.

b) Write 3 Splunk queries you would run to investigate this further. Describe what each query is looking for.

c) Write a formal hunting hypothesis based on this finding.

d) Write a Sigma rule (correct YAML) that detects msiexec.exe spawning PowerShell.

graderinfo.text:
Marking Criteria (25 points):

a) Assessment (5 pts):

  • 2 pts: Identifies relationship as suspicious (msiexec should not spawn PowerShell)
  • 2 pts: Notes count=1 significance (rare = suspicious in stack counting)
  • 1 pt: Notes immediate network connection as corroborating evidence

b) Queries (10 pts, ~3 per query + 1 logic):

  • Query 1: Should retrieve CommandLine for both processes (what was installed? what PS command ran?)
  • Query 2: Should investigate all network activity from this host/process to the suspect IP
  • Query 3: Should build a full timeline of the compromised host (what else happened?)

c) Hypothesis (5 pts):

  • Must name the technique (T1218.007 MSI installer abuse or T1059.001 PowerShell)
  • Must identify observable (msiexec spawning PS + network connection)
  • Must name data source (Sysmon EID 1, EID 3)
  • Must have time window

d) Sigma Rule (5 pts):

  • 2 pts: Valid YAML structure (title, logsource, detection, condition)
  • 1 pt: Correct logsource (process_creation, windows)
  • 1 pt: Correct selection (ParentImage endswith msiexec.exe, Image endswith powershell.exe)
  • 1 pt: ATT&CK tags and falsepositives documented

name.text: Practice Q7: MISP Event Design for VIPER-HEALTH Intrusion

questiontext.text:
You are a threat intelligence analyst at a healthcare company. After completing a threat hunt, you have confirmed:

  • Compromised system: HOSP-WS-0234
  • Initial access: Malicious LNK file in a ZIP archive sent via phishing
  • Dropper hash (SHA256): 9f8e7d6c5b4a3928374655647382910a9b8c7d6e5f4a3b2c1d0e9f8a7b6c5d4e
  • C2 IP: 91.92.248.50
  • C2 Domain: health-analytics-cdn[.]net
  • Malware: In-memory only (no persistent file after initial stage)
  • Data accessed: Patient scheduling database
  • TLP: AMBER (to be shared with H-ISAC)
  • Suspected group: VIPER-HEALTH (medium confidence, 4/5 TTP overlap)

Design the MISP event by answering all four parts:

a) Write the event metadata (info string, threat level, analysis status, distribution, TLP tag, minimum 3 ATT&CK technique tags).

b) List all attributes with: type, value, to_ids flag, and one-sentence justification for the to_ids choice.

c) Design one MISP file Object for the LNK dropper with all relevant attributes.

d) Write a YARA rule (as a MISP yara attribute) for the C2 infrastructure. Include at least 3 strings and a valid condition.

graderinfo.text:
Marking Criteria (25 points):

a) Event metadata (5 pts):

  • 1 pt: Descriptive info string (includes actor, campaign type, sector)
  • 1 pt: Threat level = High (1) or Critical
  • 1 pt: TLP:AMBER tag present
  • 2 pts: 3+ valid ATT&CK tags (T1566.001 phishing, T1204.002 user execution, T1071.001 C2 are expected; accept any valid technique)

b) Attributes (8 pts, ~1.5 per attribute):

  • ip-dst: to_ids=TRUE (block at firewall, specific IOC)
  • domain: to_ids=TRUE (block at DNS)
  • sha256 (hash): to_ids=TRUE (block in EDR)
  • LNK filename: to_ids=FALSE justification (too generic, high FP)
  • VIPER-HEALTH name: to_ids=FALSE (attribution, not detection-actionable)
  • Patient database note: to_ids=FALSE (victim context, not IOC)
  • Deduct 1pt for setting attribution/context to to_ids=TRUE

c) File Object (6 pts):

  • 2 pts: filename attribute present
  • 2 pts: sha256 attribute present with to_ids=true
  • 1 pt: mimetype = application/x-ms-shortcut or similar
  • 1 pt: comment explaining malicious nature

d) YARA Rule (6 pts):

  • 2 pts: Valid YARA structure (rule name, meta, strings, condition)
  • 2 pts: 3+ relevant strings (C2 domain, IP, and one more unique indicator)
  • 1 pt: Meaningful condition (not just 1 of them without logic)
  • 1 pt: Meta fields include tlp, author, date
